title: Osso Buco

overview.description: A classic Milanese braise of beef shin slowly cooked with vegetables, white wine and stock until the meat is meltingly tender. Finished with a fresh gremolata for brightness.

method: Season the beef shin with salt and pepper, then dust lightly with flour.
Heat the olive oil in a large casserole dish and brown the beef on both sides. Remove and set aside.
Reduce the heat and cook the onion, carrot and celery for about 10 minutes until softened.
Add the garlic and cook for 1 minute.
Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 2 minutes.
Pour in the white wine and simmer until reduced by about half.
Add the chopped tomatoes, beef stock, bay leaves and thyme.
Return the beef to the casserole, ensuring it is mostly submerged.
Cover and cook in the oven at 160°C fan (180°C conventional) for 2½–3 hours, or until the meat is fork tender.
For the gremolata, finely chop the parsley, grate the lemon zest and finely mince the remaining garlic clove. Mix together.
Remove the bay leaves and thyme stems.
Scatter the gremolata over each portion just before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Osso Buco means 'bone with a hole' in Italian, referring to the marrow-filled centre of the shin bone, which melts into the sauce during cooking.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to braise beef shin, reduce wine, and finish a rich dish with a fresh gremolata.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: An explorer recipe that introduces a classic Italian braise while using ingredients that are readily available in the UK.
chapman-tips: Ask your butcher for beef shin cut across the bone.
Don't skip the gremolata—it balances the richness of the braise beautifully.
The marrow inside the bone is perfectly edible and adds wonderful richness to the sauce.
Like many braised dishes, the flavour improves overnight.
notes.batch-cooking: Freeze cooled portions with plenty of sauce for up to three months.
notes.leftovers: Excellent reheated gently and served with fresh mashed potato or creamy polenta.
